meta.title: Saturday Questions: Backtracking in Python
meta.subtitle: Teacher replies and explanations
meta.brand: Programmers Picnic AI-ML Classes by Champak Roy

replies.title: Reply 1: Print All Binary Strings of Length n
replies.summary: The recursive function builds one string character by character. At every empty position it tries 0 and 1. When the string length becomes n, the current string is a complete answer.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.
replies.solution_code:
def binary_strings(n):
    result = []

    def backtrack(path):
        if len(path) == n:
            result.append(path)
            return

        backtrack(path + "0")
        backtrack(path + "1")

    backtrack("")
    return result

print(binary_strings(2))
print(binary_strings(3))
replies.teacher_note: Backtracking becomes easier when the student draws the decision tree and traces one branch from start to return.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the base case.
Forgetting to undo the last choice.
Using shared mutable data without copying or popping carefully.
Allowing invalid choices instead of pruning them early.

replies.title: Reply 2: Generate All Subsets of a List
replies.summary: For every element there are two choices: skip it or take it. This creates the complete power set. The path list stores the current subset, and pop is used to undo the last taken element.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.
replies.solution_code:
def subsets(nums):
    result = []

    def backtrack(index, path):
        if index == len(nums):
            result.append(path[:])
            return

        backtrack(index + 1, path)

        path.append(nums[index])
        backtrack(index + 1, path)
        path.pop()

    backtrack(0, [])
    return result

print(subsets([1, 2, 3]))
replies.teacher_note: Backtracking becomes easier when the student draws the decision tree and traces one branch from start to return.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the base case.
Forgetting to undo the last choice.
Using shared mutable data without copying or popping carefully.
Allowing invalid choices instead of pruning them early.

replies.title: Reply 3: Generate All Permutations of a String
replies.summary: A permutation is formed by choosing one unused character at every level. The used array prevents using the same position twice in one answer. After recursion returns, the character is removed from the path.
approach: Define the base case first.

Try one legal choice at a time.

replies.title: Reply 4: Generate Valid Parentheses
replies.summary: A valid parentheses string can add an opening bracket while open_count is less than n. It can add a closing bracket only when close_count is less than open_count. This rule prevents invalid prefixes.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.

replies.title: Reply 5: Combination Sum
replies.summary: The recursion tries candidates from the current index. Because the same number can be reused, the recursive call keeps the same index after choosing a candidate. If the remaining target becomes zero, the path is an answer.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.

replies.title: Reply 6: All Paths in a Maze
replies.summary: The path starts from the top-left cell and moves toward the bottom-right cell. The recursion explores legal moves and records the path when the destination is reached.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.
replies.solution_code:
def maze_paths(maze):
    result = []
    rows = len(maze)
    cols = len(maze[0])

    def backtrack(r, c, path):
        if r < 0 or c < 0 or r >= rows or c >= cols:
            return
        if maze[r][c] == 1:
            return
        if r == rows - 1 and c == cols - 1:
            result.append(path)
            return

        backtrack(r + 1, c, path + "D")
        backtrack(r, c + 1, path + "R")

    backtrack(0, 0, "")
    return result

maze = [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]
print(maze_paths(maze))
replies.teacher_note: Backtracking becomes easier when the student draws the decision tree and traces one branch from start to return.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the base case.
Forgetting to undo the last choice.
Using shared mutable data without copying or popping carefully.
Allowing invalid choices instead of pruning them early.

replies.title: Reply 7: Rat in a Maze with Four Directions
replies.summary: The rat can move in four directions. The visited matrix prevents loops. Each move is marked before recursion and unmarked after recursion so other paths can reuse the cell.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.
replies.solution_code:
def rat_in_maze(maze):
    n = len(maze)
    result = []
    visited = [[False] * n for _ in range(n)]
    moves = [(1, 0, "D"), (0, -1, "L"), (0, 1, "R"), (-1, 0, "U")]

    def backtrack(r, c, path):
        if r < 0 or c < 0 or r >= n or c >= n:
            return
        if maze[r][c] == 0 or visited[r][c]:
            return
        if r == n - 1 and c == n - 1:
            result.append(path)
            return

        visited[r][c] = True
        for dr, dc, letter in moves:
            backtrack(r + dr, c + dc, path + letter)
        visited[r][c] = False

    backtrack(0, 0, "")
    return result

maze = [[1,0,0,0],[1,1,0,1],[1,1,0,0],[0,1,1,1]]
print(rat_in_maze(maze))
replies.teacher_note: Backtracking becomes easier when the student draws the decision tree and traces one branch from start to return.

replies.title: Reply 8: Word Search in a Grid
replies.summary: The search starts from every cell. From a matching cell, depth-first search checks neighbouring cells for the next character. A visited set prevents using the same cell twice in one word path.
approach: Define the base case first.

replies.title: Reply 9: Solve the N-Queens Problem
replies.summary: Queens are placed row by row. A position is safe only if no queen is in the same column or diagonal. Sets make safety checks fast. Backtracking removes the queen before trying the next column.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.
replies.solution_code:
def solve_n_queens(n):
    result = []
    board = [["."] * n for _ in range(n)]
    cols = set()
    diag1 = set()
    diag2 = set()

    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append(["".join(r) for r in board])
            return

        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row - col) in diag1 or (row + col) in diag2:
                continue

            board[row][col] = "Q"
            cols.add(col)
            diag1.add(row - col)
            diag2.add(row + col)

            backtrack(row + 1)

            board[row][col] = "."
            cols.remove(col)
            diag1.remove(row - col)
            diag2.remove(row + col)

    backtrack(0)
    return result

print(solve_n_queens(4))
replies.teacher_note: Backtracking becomes easier when the student draws the decision tree and traces one branch from start to return.

replies.title: Reply 10: Sudoku Solver
replies.summary: Sudoku is solved by finding an empty cell, trying digits 1 to 9, checking whether each digit is valid, and recursing. When a later conflict appears, the digit is removed and the next option is tried.
approach: Define the base case first.
Store the current partial answer in a path or board.
Try one legal choice at a time.
Call the recursive function.
Undo the choice before the next iteration.
